source_name: kWh Analytics — Solar Risk Assessment 2025/2026 (fire section)
solar_wildfire_use: Wildfire claim-mix framing: 84% of fire-labeled events are equipment/brush-ignition origin (endogenous), only ~4% of events occur in high-wildfire-risk areas, 44% inverter-caused; up to 80% asset value loss range in cited cases; smoke-soiling ~6% annual revenue loss (SMOKE-SOILING scope). Splits DIRECT exogenous burnover from equipment-origin fire and from smoke-production loss.
has_open_numbers: 84% of fire events equipment/brush-ignition origin; ~4% of fire events occur in high-wildfire-risk areas; 44% inverter-caused; up to 80% asset value loss (case range); ~6% avg annual revenue loss from smoke-soiling; 60% of portfolios saw double-digit generation loss in 2-week smoke windows.
does_not_have_open: Isolated $/MW physical severity for exogenous wildfire burnover of a utility solar array; capacity/TIV denominators for the fire-cause split.
notes: The 84% equipment/brush-origin share is critical: most 'solar fire' losses are endogenous, NOT exogenous wildfire burnover (the scope of the canonical wildfire_solar cell). Do not conflate. DIRECT (84%/4%/44%/80%) and SMOKE-SOILING (6%/60%) figures kept as separate metric families. SCOPE CAVEAT: all-cause PV fire, blends endogenous+exogenous ignition; NOT an exogenous-wildfire benchmark — cannot validate a landscape-wildfire model.

source_name: kWh Analytics — Solar Risk Assessment loss database, hail/fire peril split (webinar slide)
solar_wildfire_use: Contextualizes fire vs hail share of the kWh loss database: fire = 4.81%/8.41% of losses (OCR-ambiguous which is count vs $ share); denominator is 13,000+ asset-years, ~$100B TIV, 92 GW, 2012-2025, $1.5B total ground-up losses.
has_open_numbers: Fire = 4.81% or 8.41% of database losses (column/axis OCR-ambiguous, carry both readings); denominator: 13,000+ asset-years, ~$100B TIV, 92 GW DC, 2012-2025, $1.5B cumulative ground-up losses.
does_not_have_open: Unambiguous count-vs-dollar attribution of the 4.81%/8.41% split; wildfire isolated from equipment-origin fire within this figure; per-MW severity.
notes: Same portfolio family as VDE_AXIS_PERIL_MIX_2025-style denominators (13,000+ asset-years/92GW/$100B TIV/$1.5B ground-up) used elsewhere in the flood dossier; OCR ambiguity in the source slide means both 4.81% and 8.41% must be carried forward, not averaged. SCOPE CAVEAT: all-cause PV fire, blends endogenous+exogenous ignition; NOT an exogenous-wildfire benchmark — cannot validate a landscape-wildfire model.

source_name: GCube — Cell Interrupted / NA claims report (2016 vintage + later ~2023 update)
solar_wildfire_use: Fires = 16% of claims / 20% of total costs in a 5-year window ending ~2023; an earlier 2016 report cites weather ~50% of NA claims (context, not wildfire-isolated).
has_open_numbers: Fires = 16% of claims by count, 20% of total costs, 5-yr window ending ~2023; earlier (2016) figure: weather ~50% of North American solar claims.
does_not_have_open: Isolation of wildfire from equipment-origin/other fire causes within the 16%/20% figures; per-MW severity; denominator/portfolio size.
notes: The 16%/20% 'fires' bucket almost certainly blends equipment-origin and exogenous wildfire causes (per the kWh 84%-equipment-origin finding) — treat as an upper bound on wildfire's true share, not a wildfire-isolated figure. SCOPE CAVEAT: all-cause PV fire, blends endogenous+exogenous ignition; NOT an exogenous-wildfire benchmark — cannot validate a landscape-wildfire model.

source_name: GCube — Hail or High Water (solar extreme-weather claims report)
solar_wildfire_use: Roughly 50% of solar extreme-weather claim value attributed to wildfire, characterized as 'tens of millions' cumulative over the decade — an aggregate order-of-magnitude figure, not per-MW. Also cross-listed/corroborated in R3 with an S&P Global figure that wildfire+windstorm+tornado = 80% of claims over the past 10 years and $300M in claims since 2015 (combined, not wildfire-isolated).
has_open_numbers: ~50% of solar extreme-weather claims = wildfire; 'tens of millions of dollars' cumulative wildfire losses over the decade; wildfire cited as historically the most frequent solar damage cause among extreme-weather perils; corroborating S&P Global figure (via same GCube dataset lineage): wildfire+windstorm+tornado = 80% of claims past 10 years, $300M in claims since 2015 (combined bucket).
does_not_have_open: Per-MW severity; separation of DIRECT burnover from smoke/soiling revenue loss; isolated wildfire-only dollar figure (only combined-bucket figures are open); project/portfolio denominators.
notes: Wildfire claim-share estimates vary ~5x across insurers (GCube ~50% vs kWh's ~4% of events in high-risk areas vs AXIS's third-party-cited 23%). DO NOT average across sources — different denominators, time windows, and definitions (weather-claims-only vs all-cause vs NatCat-only). SCOPE CAVEAT: all-cause PV fire, blends endogenous+exogenous ignition; NOT an exogenous-wildfire benchmark — cannot validate a landscape-wildfire model.

source_name: GCube — North America NatCat market update (2022, Texas hail-dominated)
solar_wildfire_use: $300M+ Texas hail-dominated NatCat losses in 2022; sublimits up to $50M breached. Wildfire not separately broken out here, but same GCube report family is used elsewhere for wildfire commentary (see GCUBE_TX_HAIL_WILDFIRE_COMPARISON_2023).
has_open_numbers: $300M+ NatCat losses in Texas, 2022 (hail-dominated); sublimits up to $50M breached.
does_not_have_open: Any wildfire-specific isolation within this report; the $300M figure is hail-attributed, not wildfire.
notes: Included as denominator/context for the GCube report family and to support the GCUBE_TX_HAIL_WILDFIRE_COMPARISON_2023 cross-reference; not itself a wildfire number.

source_name: GCube — Known Unknowns (2025 renewable NatCat market report)
solar_wildfire_use: US NatCat events >$300M including hail and wildfire bundled together (not isolated); general market commentary on rising renewable NatCat severity.
has_open_numbers: US NatCat events >$300M (incl. hail and wildfire, not separated); qualitative market-hardening commentary.
does_not_have_open: Wildfire-isolated $ figure; per-MW severity; full report is largely gated beyond press-cited figures.
notes: Partial open (press-cited figures) + HAS_PATHWAY_ONLY for the full report; wildfire bundled inside a >$300M generic NatCat bucket. SCOPE CAVEAT: all-cause PV fire, blends endogenous+exogenous ignition; NOT an exogenous-wildfire benchmark — cannot validate a landscape-wildfire model.

source_name: GCube — McLachlan interview (trade press, 2025)
solar_wildfire_use: Key qualitative admission: GCube has NOT yet had a major utility-scale solar wildfire loss; California cannot exclude wildfire coverage but can sublimit or price for it.
has_open_numbers: Qualitative only, no $: GCube has not had a major utility-scale solar wildfire loss to date; in California, wildfire cannot be excluded from renewable policies but can be sublimited/priced.
does_not_have_open: Any dollar figure whatsoever — this source is qualitative/narrative only.
notes: Directly informs the Golden Rule for this pair: even a leading renewable insurer's own underwriter states no major realized utility-scale solar wildfire loss has occurred yet, which explains the overall scarcity of open wildfire-isolated $/MW figures across all source classes. SCOPE CAVEAT: all-cause PV fire, blends endogenous+exogenous ignition; NOT an exogenous-wildfire benchmark — cannot validate a landscape-wildfire model.

source_name: AXIS Capital — solar vs hail claims report (2025, primary report)
solar_wildfire_use: Ranks perils for solar: NA hail ~55% of gross claims, wildfire 'shortly behind' (no % given); Global: hail 27% of gross claim $, tornado/wind 23% of frequency / 45% of $; downtime days by cause; $/MW figures given are for hail/stow scenarios, not wildfire.
has_open_numbers: NA: hail ~55% of gross claims, wildfire ranked next (no %); Global: hail 27% of gross claim $, wind/tornado 23% freq / 45% $; $380k/MW (tracker stow-failure, hail-caused, NOT wildfire), $340k/MW fixed-tilt (hail), $150k/MW successful-stow (hail); ~240 days avg downtime for NatCat/extreme-weather events (includes wildfire in the aggregate category but not isolated).
does_not_have_open: Wildfire-only percentage or $/MW figure — all quantified $/MW figures in this report are hail-specific.
notes: The $150-380k/MW figures are HAIL-caused, not wildfire-caused — must not be relabeled as wildfire severity. Wildfire is only qualitatively ranked ('shortly behind' hail in NA), with no isolated $ or % figure in the primary report. SCOPE CAVEAT: all-cause PV fire, blends endogenous+exogenous ignition; NOT an exogenous-wildfire benchmark — cannot validate a landscape-wildfire model.

source_name: AXIS Capital data as reported by pv magazine (July 2025 vintage)
solar_wildfire_use: Third-party citation of AXIS data: wildfire = 23%, tornado = 18%, hail = 55% of NatCat/extreme-weather solar claims (July 2025 vintage), a different cut than AXIS's own global 27%-hail figure.
has_open_numbers: Wildfire = 23%, tornado = 18%, hail = 55% of NatCat/extreme-weather solar claims (as reported by pv magazine, July 2025).
does_not_have_open: Independent primary-source confirmation of the 23% figure directly from AXIS (only found via secondary trade-press citation); denominator/vintage reconciliation against AXIS's own 27%-hail global figure.
notes: Flagged low-to-medium confidence: this 23% wildfire figure could not be directly corroborated in AXIS's own primary report text retrieved for AXIS_SOLAR_VS_HAIL_2025, which instead only says wildfire is 'shortly behind' hail in NA without a percentage. Possible vintage/geography mismatch (NA vs global cut) — do not average with other AXIS figures. SCOPE CAVEAT: all-cause PV fire, blends endogenous+exogenous ignition; NOT an exogenous-wildfire benchmark — cannot validate a landscape-wildfire model.

source_name: Marsh — renewable-energy NatCat claim materials
solar_wildfire_use: NatCat (storm+hail named explicitly) = 80% of solar PV claim value globally; wildfire not separately isolated, sits inside the residual/NatCat bucket.
has_open_numbers: NatCat = 80% of solar PV claim value globally (storm+hail explicitly named as the dominant components).
does_not_have_open: Wildfire-only quantification; per-MW figure; DIRECT vs smoke split.
notes: Aggregate-only figure; wildfire sits inside the NatCat bucket alongside storm and hail, which are explicitly named as dominant — wildfire's residual share within the 80% is unknown and should not be assumed to be large. SCOPE CAVEAT: all-cause PV fire, blends endogenous+exogenous ignition; NOT an exogenous-wildfire benchmark — cannot validate a landscape-wildfire model.

source_name: Gallagher Re — Insuring the Energy Transition (2024 report)
solar_wildfire_use: Names wildfire qualitatively as one of the 'biggest losses' drivers for solar; no wildfire-specific % or $ figure given in the open report.
has_open_numbers: Qualitative: wildfire named as a top loss driver for solar assets.
does_not_have_open: Any wildfire-specific % or $ figure; the quantified version of this claim (if it exists) is gated.
notes: HAS_PATHWAY_ONLY for the quantified wildfire share; open text is qualitative narrative only. SCOPE CAVEAT: all-cause PV fire, blends endogenous+exogenous ignition; NOT an exogenous-wildfire benchmark — cannot validate a landscape-wildfire model.

source_name: 60Hertz Energy — FLARE (Fire Linked Asset Risk Evaluation) model, as published in kWh Analytics 2025 SRA
solar_wildfire_use: SMOKE-SOILING scope: ~6% average annual revenue loss (subset of 40 sites with generation loss, ~1.2 GW(?) capacity, FLARE model using Aerosol Optical Depth); extreme case: 60% of a national portfolio saw double-digit generation loss during specific 2-week smoke-event windows.
has_open_numbers: 6% avg annual site revenue loss (subset with generation loss, n=40 sites, ~1.2 GW(?) capacity, Western/Southeast/Northeast US); 60% of a developer's national portfolio saw double-digit (>10%) generation loss during 2-week smoke-event windows; this finding 'doubles' an implied ~3% 2022 SRA baseline.
does_not_have_open: $/MWh or absolute $ cleaning-cost figure; confirmation of exact unit for '1.2 Gwh' (likely capacity typo, not energy); independently verified 2022 SRA baseline.
notes: The single most citable open number for the SMOKE-SOILING metric family in the entire R1 sweep. Strictly SMOKE-SOILING (generation/revenue loss), never to be blended with DIRECT physical-damage figures. Portfolio-bias caveat: 40-site convenience sample of contributor-provided sites.

source_name: GCube (via Insurance Journal) — Texas hail vs wildfire+storms+flooding comparison
solar_wildfire_use: 2022 Texas hail losses (~$300M) were 'nearly twice' the financial hit from 'severe storms, wildfires and flooding' at notable US wind/solar facilities since 2020 — implies a combined bucket of ~$150M (half of $300M), not a wildfire-isolated figure.
has_open_numbers: Texas hailstorms caused ~$300M in losses (2022); this was 'nearly twice' the combined hit from severe storms + wildfires + flooding at US wind/solar facilities since 2020 (implying ≈$150M combined, derived); insurers capping coverage at $10M in high-risk areas; solar property insurance premiums up as much as 50% year-over-year.
does_not_have_open: Wildfire isolated from the combined storms+wildfire+flooding bucket; per-MW severity; portfolio denominator.
notes: The ~$150M figure is a DERIVED implied ceiling for a combined 'storms+wildfire+flooding' bucket (half of the stated $300M hail figure), not a directly-stated wildfire-only number — must be flagged as derived and low confidence. SCOPE CAVEAT: all-cause PV fire, blends endogenous+exogenous ignition; NOT an exogenous-wildfire benchmark — cannot validate a landscape-wildfire model.

source_name: J.S. Held — forensic/fire-investigation practice (pathway only; no solar-wildfire case study located)
solar_wildfire_use: Documented as an outreach/access pathway; no public J.S. Held (or Sedgwick) report specifically addressing utility-scale solar PV wildfire damage was located via open search.
has_open_numbers: None found relevant to utility-scale solar wildfire.
does_not_have_open: Any open numeric data — no public solar-wildfire-specific report was located from this firm or Sedgwick.
notes: Pathway: direct outreach to J.S. Held's Renewable Energy or Fire & Explosion Investigation practice group (or Sedgwick) to request anonymized/aggregated case studies of utility-scale solar PV wildfire-origin or wildfire-exposure claims, cause-and-origin findings, and repair-cost breakdowns by component — likely requires NDA/paid licensing. SCOPE CAVEAT: all-cause PV fire, blends endogenous+exogenous ignition; NOT an exogenous-wildfire benchmark — cannot validate a landscape-wildfire model.

source_name: Moody's RMS — North America Wildfire HD Model v2.0
solar_wildfire_use: Not solar-specific (an Industrial Facilities specialty model exists within the RMS suite, but no published solar-PV loss costs). Open context numbers only: LA firestorm insured-loss range $20B-$30B (all-property, not solar); SCE wildfire-mitigation case study $1B/1yr and $3.5B/3yr avoided losses (utility T&D infrastructure, not solar generation).
has_open_numbers: No open AAL/PML numbers for solar. Open context: LA firestorm insured-loss range $20B-$30B (all-property); SCE mitigation case study: $1B avoided losses over 1 year (55% reduction), $3.5B avoided over 3 years (65% reduction) — utility infrastructure, not solar.
does_not_have_open: Any solar-specific AAL/PML output — the model's solar-relevant loss costs are proprietary/gated behind an RMS license.
notes: SCE figures are for utility T&D (transmission & distribution) infrastructure hardening, NOT solar generation assets — do not relabel as solar. LA firestorm $20-30B is all-property, residential-dominated.

source_name: Verisk/AIR — Wildfire Model for the United States
solar_wildfire_use: Not solar-specific. AAL defined methodologically as expected loss per year averaged over 10,000 simulated years. Open context: largest insured wildfire loss cited is $14B (2017); LA firestorm estimate $28B-$35B (all-property).
has_open_numbers: AAL methodology definition: expected loss per year averaged over 10,000 simulated years. Largest insured wildfire loss cited: $14B (2017). LA firestorm (Jan 2025) estimate: $28B-$35B (all-property).
does_not_have_open: Any solar-specific AAL/PML output — gated behind an AIR/Verisk license.
notes: All open figures are all-property (not solar-segmented); useful only as macro AAL-methodology and loss-magnitude context, never as a solar benchmark.

source_name: PCS (Verisk) — Wildfire Catastrophe Designations
solar_wildfire_use: Industry-wide, not solar-segmented, but provides open named-event industry-loss bulletins useful for macro context/ceiling checks: Fort McMurray 2016 C$4.6B (~US$3.52B); historical count of 23 wildland-fire CAT events since 1949 totaling >$8B (>$5B in the most recent 10 years, as of 2013); Palisades+Eaton Fires (Jan 2025) preliminary $28B-$35B, later raised to $35.7B by April 2025; PCS designation minimum threshold = $25,000,000.
has_open_numbers: Fort McMurray 2016: C$4.6B (~US$3.52B). 23 wildland-fire CAT events since 1949, >$8B cumulative, >$5B in most recent 10 yrs (as of 2013). Palisades+Eaton (Jan 2025): preliminary $28B-$35B, raised to $35.7B by April 2025 ($20.6B Palisades, $15.1B Eaton). 2003 San Diego/San Bernardino wildfires: $2.04B. PCS designation threshold: $25,000,000 minimum industry loss.
does_not_have_open: Any solar-segmented loss figure — PCS designations are industry-wide (all lines of business), not asset-class-specific; underlying claims methodology is gated.
notes: Industry-wide (all-property/all-lines) bulletins, useful only as a macro event-loss ceiling/context reference — never a solar-specific benchmark.

source_name: Renew Risk — renewable-energy-specific catastrophe model
solar_wildfire_use: Solar farms named as a covered asset class ('only commercially available cat models built specifically for renewable energy assets'), but wildfire is not explicitly named as a modeled peril on the public product page and no open numeric loss costs are published.
has_open_numbers: None open — product description only.
does_not_have_open: Confirmation that wildfire is even a modeled peril in this product; any numeric loss-cost, AAL, or PML figures.
notes: Uncertain wildfire coverage: the public page does not explicitly list wildfire among modeled perils, unlike hail/wind — flagged as a pathway to inquire about directly rather than assumed to cover wildfire.

source_name: VDE Americas — Natural Catastrophe Risk & Insurability Report
solar_wildfire_use: Solar-only company; wildfire is listed as one of five perils a NatCat model 'must characterize' for solar insurability, but with zero methodology or figures disclosed publicly. VDE's demonstrated expertise/quantification is concentrated in hail, not wildfire.
has_open_numbers: None — wildfire listed only as a peril category name, no figures.
does_not_have_open: Any wildfire-specific numeric figure whatsoever.
notes: HAS_PATHWAY_ONLY for any underlying wildfire methodology; VDE's public quantitative output is hail-focused, and wildfire is currently only a named category, not a quantified peril, on the public materials reviewed.

source_name: ZestyAI — Z-FIRE wildfire risk model
solar_wildfire_use: Not solar-specific (residential/commercial property lines; no utility-scale solar case study found). Open capability numbers only: trained on 1,500+/2,000+/1,200+ historical wildfire events (figure varies by citation); 44x lift vs regional models; 97-100% US property coverage; Palisades/Eaton fire zones scored 94%/87% High-or-Very-High risk; vegetation study found 78% destruction rate with heavy near-structure vegetation vs 1.9x lower with managed vegetation. No $ loss-cost or AAL numbers published.
has_open_numbers: Trained on 1,500+ / 2,000+ / 1,200+ historical fires (varies by citation); 44x lift vs regional models; 97-100% US property coverage; Palisades/Eaton zones scored 94%/87% High-or-Very-High risk; vegetation study: 78% destruction rate with heavy near-structure vegetation vs. 1.9x lower with managed vegetation.
does_not_have_open: Any $ loss-cost, AAL, or PML figure; any utility-scale solar-specific validation case; the Z-SPARK non-weather-fire $25B headline is deferred as equipment-origin, not wildfire.
notes: Residential/commercial property model with no confirmed utility-scale solar use case; property-count/coverage and model-lift statistics are open, but no solar-relevant dollar figures exist publicly.

source_name: Descartes Underwriting — parametric wildfire ('Fire-in-a-Circle' / 'Burnt Area')
solar_wildfire_use: Renewable energy/solar explicitly listed as a covered industry. Open product-structure numbers: two trigger structures (radius-based 'Fire-in-a-Circle', hectares-burned 'Burnt Area'); capacity up to $100 million per contract. Pricing/loss-cost figures are gated. No solar-specific $/MW loss-cost published.
has_open_numbers: Two trigger structures: 'Fire-in-a-Circle' (proximity/radius-based) and 'Burnt Area' (hectares burned). Capacity up to $100 million per contract. Solar/renewable energy explicitly listed as covered industry.
does_not_have_open: Solar-specific $/MW loss-cost or pricing figures — gated behind Descartes' underwriting process.

source_name: Arbol — parametric wildfire product
solar_wildfire_use: Not explicitly solar-branded (branded for homes/businesses; renewables covered under Arbol's broader energy book, but the wildfire product itself is not solar-branded). Open structure: boundary-crossing trigger via NIFC/Cal Fire perimeter data; limits $1M-$10M; proof-of-loss minimum $10,000; payout within 10 business days.
has_open_numbers: Trigger: fire crossing insured-property boundary per NIFC/Cal Fire perimeter data. Limits: $1M-$10M. Proof-of-loss minimum: $10,000. Payout within 10 business days.
does_not_have_open: Any solar-specific pricing, loss-cost, or utilization data.
notes: Structure is open and generic (not solar-branded specifically) — treat as a product-design reference/pathway, not a solar-specific benchmark.

source_name: California Valley Solar Ranch (Clearway) fire, San Luis Obispo Co., CA
solar_wildfire_use: DIRECT physical-damage case: $8-9M estimated loss; 84% of plant capacity knocked out; 1,127 acres burned; 250 MW plant. Cause-coding ambiguous — likely bird/electrical-arc ignition on-site rather than an exogenous wildfire spreading into the site; no module damage reported, only poles/cabling.
has_open_numbers: $8-9M estimated loss; 84% capacity knocked out; 1,127 acres burned; 250 MW nameplate capacity.
does_not_have_open: Confirmed cause-of-ignition classification (equipment/avian-arc vs true exogenous wildfire spread); MW-normalized $/MW figure (250 MW denominator exists but 84%-capacity-affected vs total-site-value basis is unclear); module-level damage breakdown (poles/cabling only, per source).
notes: CAUSE_CODING_AMBIGUOUS: likely avian/electrical-arc ignition, not classic exogenous wildfire-into-site. $8-9M / 250MW implies ~$32-36k/MW if naively divided, but this is a derived sensitivity, not a stated per-MW figure, and the damage was concentrated in poles/cabling (not module loss) — flag any derived $/MW clearly as illustrative.

source_name: DEPCOM Power case study — unnamed 20 MW plant, Kern County, CA, wildfire recovery
solar_wildfire_use: $2.6M + $1.4M restoration savings; $1.2M (or $400K, source-inconsistent) avoided lost revenue; 60 days faster restoration versus a competitor baseline; ~1,000 modules destroyed. This is a savings-vs-competitor framing, not a gross incurred loss figure.
has_open_numbers: $2.6M + $1.4M restoration cost savings (vs. competitor baseline); $1.2M avoided lost revenue (also cited elsewhere in the same case study as $400K — internally inconsistent); 60 days faster restoration; ~1,000 modules destroyed; 20 MW plant.
does_not_have_open: A clean gross-incurred-loss figure (only savings-vs-competitor and avoided-loss framing given, which are not the same as incurred damage); reconciliation of the $1.2M vs $400K avoided-revenue inconsistency.
notes: Source is internally inconsistent on the avoided-revenue figure ($1.2M vs $400K cited in different places) — carry both, flagged. Savings/avoided-loss framing is NOT the same metric family as a gross incurred loss; do not treat as a clean severity benchmark.

source_name: Canyon Fire 2 grid/solar trip, Anaheim Hills, Orange County, CA
solar_wildfire_use: ~900 MW of solar capacity tripped off near-instantaneously due to a subcycle overvoltage fault triggered by the fire — a grid/inverter-trip event, not physical burn damage. Figure is uncited on its source (Wikipedia) and should be treated as unverified/gated pending primary-source confirmation.
has_open_numbers: ~900 MW of solar capacity tripped off near-instantaneously (subcycle overvoltage fault).
does_not_have_open: Primary-source citation for the 900 MW figure (currently only found uncited on a secondary reference page); any dollar-loss or duration figure for the trip event.
notes: This is a grid-stability/inverter-trip event (momentary generation loss), fundamentally different from physical fire damage to equipment — do not conflate with DIRECT physical-damage figures. Source citation is weak (uncited secondary reference).

source_name: Wellington North Solar Farm (Lightsource bp) fire, NSW, Australia
solar_wildfire_use: 400-425 MW farm; 92 ha grass fire on-site; operating at ~90% capacity post-fire; no damage to major equipment reported. Illustrates a case where a fire event caused minimal-to-no physical loss.
has_open_numbers: 400-425 MW farm; 92 ha grass fire; operating at ~90% capacity post-fire; no major equipment damage reported.
does_not_have_open: Any dollar figure (none disclosed); confirmation of exact capacity (400 vs 425 MW cited variously).
notes: A near-zero-physical-loss case despite a fire event on-site — useful as a lower-bound anchor alongside VALDORA_SOLAR_ZERO_LOSS_2022-style zero-loss anchors in the flood dossier, but no $ figure exists to record as a benchmark row.

source_name: Beryl Solar Farm (RES Australia / Banpu Energy) fire, Gulgong, NSW
solar_wildfire_use: 110 MW farm; fire burned under panels; site fully shut down and isolated; no dollar or downtime figure disclosed.
has_open_numbers: 110 MW farm; fire burned under panels; site shut down/isolated.
does_not_have_open: Any dollar, downtime, or damage-extent figure.
notes: No open numeric data beyond MW capacity and a qualitative description — included for completeness of the named-event catalog, not usable as a benchmark.

source_name: Raywood Solar Farm (Sungrow) inverter fire, Victoria, Australia
solar_wildfire_use: 20 MW farm; fire broke out in a Sungrow inverter and spread a short way into long grass under the panels; forced regulatory shutdown until vegetation is cut back below 100mm. Equipment-origin ignition that then threatens to spread as a fire (PV-as-ignition-source), the inverse of wildfire-into-site — noted but kept scope-separate.
has_open_numbers: 20 MW capacity; fire started in inverter, spread into grass under panels; ten fire units responded ~5:50pm, under control by 8:30pm; forced shutdown until vegetation managed below 100mm.
does_not_have_open: Any dollar figure (revenue loss during outage undisclosed); precise duration of the shutdown as of retrieval.
notes: CAUSE_CODING_AMBIGUOUS: this is PV-equipment-as-ignition-source (inverter fire spreading to vegetation), the inverse of the canonical wildfire-into-site peril — must not be relabeled as exogenous wildfire damage.

source_name: Mannum 2 Solar Farm (Epic Energy) inverter fire, South Australia
solar_wildfire_use: ~$250,000 damage estimate (currency unconfirmed, likely AUD) from an inverter-in-shipping-container fire; crews kept the fire contained to the shipping container, preventing spread to nearby panels; one contractor injured. Equipment-origin, not wildfire-into-site.
has_open_numbers: ~$250,000 damage bill estimate; fire contained to shipping container (did not spread to panels); 1 contractor injured; project was being commissioned at the time.
does_not_have_open: MW capacity of the Mannum 2 project (not stated in source, so no per-MW normalization possible); confirmed currency (AUD assumed, unconfirmed).
notes: CAUSE_CODING_AMBIGUOUS: containerized-inverter/BESS-adjacent equipment fire, not an exogenous wildfire burning into the site — useful only as a lower-bound severity anchor for equipment-fire losses, not a wildfire-into-site DIRECT benchmark. Currency (AUD) unconfirmed but implied by South Australian source.

source_name: Energy Safe Victoria — 5 Sungrow-owned solar farms shut down over bushfire/vegetation risk
solar_wildfire_use: Regulatory order to cease generation at 5 Sungrow-owned farms (Raywood, Goornong, Ledcourt, Stawell, Moolort) effective 22 Feb 2025, citing vegetation-management/bushfire-ignition risk following the Raywood fire; no dollar figure disclosed; regulatory/preventive shutdown, not a realized loss.
has_open_numbers: 5 solar farms ordered to cease generating electricity, effective midday 22 Feb 2025; shutdown to remain until Energy Safe is satisfied vegetation is managed appropriately; no aggregate MW figure for the 5-farm group disclosed (only Raywood's 20 MW known).
does_not_have_open: Any dollar figure for lost revenue during the shutdown; aggregate MW capacity across all 5 farms; final shutdown-end date as of retrieval.
notes: Regulatory/preventive shutdown due to vegetation-management-driven bushfire-ignition risk, not an incurred physical-damage loss — valuable frequency/regulatory context, not a severity benchmark.

source_name: 2020 California wildfire smoke — CAISO solar generation drop (Reuters/EIA)
solar_wildfire_use: SMOKE-SOILING scope: ~30% generation drop vs. the July 2020 average; 13.4% year-over-year decline in the first half of September 2020 despite +659 MW / +5% utility-scale capacity growth in California.

source_name: NREL — 'Impact of Wildfires on Solar Generation, Reserves and Energy Prices' (technical report, CAISO focus)
solar_wildfire_use: SMOKE-SOILING scope: ~450 MW average generation decrease in September 2020; up to 7.7% monthly PV generation reduction; operating reserve requirements up 53-64%; AOD increase from ~0.10 background to 0.53 peak in September; ~1,440,000 acres burned in CA in 2020.

source_name: NCAR/UCAR study — smoke effect on California solar peak-hour output, 2020
solar_wildfire_use: SMOKE-SOILING scope: 10-30% peak-hour (noon-4pm) production decline on heavy-smoke days; <7% decline on low-smoke days; up to 50% below forecast in late-afternoon/evening on the smokiest days; 27% average below forecast on the smokiest days.

solar_wildfire_use: SMOKE-SOILING scope: mean 4.1% generation decrease across 20 Australian solar farms (Dec 2019-Jan 2020); regional breakdown: ACT 10.3%, NSW 4.1%, VIC 3.5%, SA 1.2%; individual sites: Mugga Lane Solar Park 12.0% (peak), Royalla Solar Farm 8.6%.

source_name: DOE/FEMP — PVROM dataset, western US wildfire smoke impact on PV production
solar_wildfire_use: SMOKE-SOILING scope: 9.4%-37.8% measured power-production drop across PVROM sites in the western US; California statewide decline of 10-30%, up to 58% locally (September 2020). Also documents Class A fire rating guidance (UL 1703) relevant to DIRECT/mechanism scope.
has_open_numbers: 9.4%-37.8% measured power-production drop (PVROM western US sites); CA statewide decline 10-30%, up to 58% locally (Sept 2020); Class A fire rating guidance per UL 1703 referenced.

Cross-corroborated with the same 9.4-37.8% figure independently reported in R4 (OSTI_GILLETLY / DOE_FEMP_SOLAR_PV_WILDFIRE_HARDENING) — same underlying PVROM dataset, not independent confirmation.

source_name: Kings County, CA solar panel field grass fire (ABC30/KFSN)
solar_wildfire_use: 267 acres burned near a solar panel field in Kings County, CA; cause under investigation; no solar farm name, MW capacity, or dollar figure disclosed.

does_not_have_open: Solar farm name; MW capacity; $ damage; equipment-loss detail.

source_name: Yang et al. 2015 — materials study of PV module flammability (cone calorimeter)
solar_wildfire_use: DIRECT mechanism scope: critical heat flux (CHF) = 26 kW/m^2 for PV module ignition (single-PV-cell module, cone calorimeter); ignition times 913s (at 28 kW/m^2) down to 83s (at 45 kW/m^2); total heat release 38-57 MJ/m^2; peak heat release rate 85 to 402 kW/m^2.
has_open_numbers: CHF = 26 kW/m^2; ignition times 913s->83s (28->45 kW/m^2); total heat release 38-57 MJ/m^2; peak HRR 85->402 kW/m^2.
does_not_have_open: Any financial/dollar figure — this is a pure materials-science mechanism study, not a loss dataset.
notes: This is the most-cited open CHF figure for PV modules (26 kW/m^2), corroborated independently by WANG_WANG_ZHAO_SEMSC_2025_PVGLASS. DIRECT/mechanism scope only — no $ figures. This is a hazard-intensity mechanism datum (ignition threshold), not a loss benchmark; provides the physical basis for an intensity axis, not a dollar severity.

source_name: Wang, Wang & Zhao 2025 (SEMSC) — PV glass ignition study
solar_wildfire_use: DIRECT mechanism scope: CHF = 26 kW/m^2 for single-glass/double-glazed PV module, corroborating the Yang 2015 figure. Note: this is the closest verified real analog found for a target citation ('Wang 2025, Int J Thermal Sciences 209:109489') that could not itself be independently verified as stated.
has_open_numbers: CHF = 26 kW/m^2 (single-glass/double-glazed PV module) — abstract-level extraction only.
does_not_have_open: Full-text verification beyond the abstract; confirmation this matches the originally-targeted 'Int J Thermal Sciences 209:109489' citation (flagged as unverified/possible mismatch).

Corroborates Yang 2015's 26 kW/m^2 CHF figure independently.

source_name: SSRN preprint (2024) — glass/PV ignition and heat-transfer study
solar_wildfire_use: DIRECT mechanism scope: qualitative ignition/heat-transfer findings only (abstract-level extraction), no additional quantified CHF beyond what is corroborated elsewhere. Flagged as the closest match found for a target citation ('Zhao 2026, Fire 9(2):62') that could not itself be verified as stated.

does_not_have_open: Full-text quantified figures; confirmation this matches the originally-targeted 'Zhao 2026, Fire 9(2):62' citation (flagged as unverified/possible mismatch).
notes: FLAGGED: could not verify the originally-requested citation ('Zhao 2026, Fire 9(2):62') as stated; documented as the closest verified real analog with an explicit discrepancy note.

source_name: Zhang et al. 2022 (Energies journal) — XLPE cable flammability study
solar_wildfire_use: DIRECT mechanism scope: CHF = 16.24 kW/m^2 for XLPE cable (cone calorimeter) — notably lower than the ~26 kW/m^2 PV-module CHF, implying cabling may ignite before module glass in a real fire exposure. Ignition times 83.5s (at 20 kW/m^2) down to 13.3s (at 40 kW/m^2); thermal response parameter (TRP) = 325.7 kW*s^0.5/m^2.
does_not_have_open: Any financial/dollar figure; XLPE-cable-specific claims/loss data.
url: https://doi.org/10.3390/en15134693 (approximate DOI path for Energies journal article; verify via publisher)
notes: Cross-material comparison datum: cabling (16.24 kW/m^2 CHF) ignites at a lower heat flux than PV module glass (~26 kW/m^2), a mechanism-relevant finding for damage-curve sequencing, not a loss figure.

source_name: USFS Missoula Fire Sciences Lab — FSim national burn-probability + flame-length-probability dataset (270m), 3rd Edition
solar_wildfire_use: This is the canonical hazard-axis denominator named explicitly in the brief: 6 discrete flame-length-probability bins (FLP1: <2ft, FLP2: 2-4ft, FLP3: 4-6ft, FLP4: 6-8ft, FLP5: 8-12ft, FLP6: 12+ft), CONUS+AK+HI, 270m resolution, landscape conditions as of end of 2020 (3rd edition, 2023 publication). Pure probabilistic hazard-simulation output, no dollar-loss data.
has_open_numbers: Burn probability (BP) raster + 6 flame-length-probability bins: FLP1 <2ft, FLP2 2-4ft, FLP3 4-6ft, FLP4 6-8ft, FLP5 8-12ft, FLP6 12+ft; CONUS+AK+HI, 270m grid, landscape conditions end of 2020.
does_not_have_open: Any dollar-loss data of any kind; solar-facility attribute (requires independent GIS join against USPVDB).
notes: The canonical hazard-axis intensity variable (flame-length-probability bins) for any future solar-wildfire damage curve, analogous to flood-depth for flood_solar. Pure hazard/probability layer — DENOMINATOR_ONLY, no loss figures. Cross-referenced identically in R5 as source #4 (same dataset, exposure-research angle).

source_name: FARSITE / Byram fireline-intensity to flame-length conversion formulas
solar_wildfire_use: DIRECT mechanism scope: Byram's fireline intensity formula I_b = h*w*R/60 (kW/m); flame-length conversion L_f = 0.0775*I_b^0.46 (Byram) or 0.237*I_B^0.46 (Albini); CSIRO recalibration L_f = 0.04001*I_f^0.5846 (forest) / 0.03888*I_f^0.5111 (grassland). Formulas only, no site-specific loss data.
has_open_numbers: I_b = h*w*R/60 (Byram fireline intensity, kW/m); L_f = 0.0775*I_b^0.46 (Byram flame-length) / 0.237*I_B^0.46 (Albini); CSIRO: L_f = 0.04001*I_f^0.5846 (forest) / 0.03888*I_f^0.5111 (grassland).
does_not_have_open: Any dollar or loss figure; site-specific application to a solar facility.
url: https://www.fs.usda.gov/rmrs/ (FARSITE/BehavePlus documentation; exact PDF pending verification)
notes: Pure engineering formula/methodology reference converting fireline intensity to flame length — the mechanistic link between the USFS_FSIM burn-probability/FLP hazard axis and a physical flame-length damage input. No financial figures.

source_name: NIST Technical Note 1796 — Witch and Guejito Fires forensic case study
solar_wildfire_use: DIRECT mechanism scope, not PV-specific: 274 homes in the fire's path, 245 within the final perimeter, 74 destroyed / 16 damaged in the Rancho Bernardo area specifically; Witch Fire overall totals 1,125 destroyed / 77 damaged. Full quantitative tables were not re-extracted from the primary PDF in this research pass (relied on secondary program pages/news/slide decks) — flagged HAS_PATHWAY_ONLY/partial.
has_open_numbers: 274 homes in fire path, 245 within final perimeter, 74 destroyed/16 damaged (Rancho Bernardo); Witch Fire overall: 1,125 destroyed/77 damaged.
does_not_have_open: Full primary-PDF quantitative tables (percentage/table-level figures) — recommend a follow-up direct fetch of the primary NIST TN 1796 PDF; any PV-specific application (this is a residential WUI case study).
notes: Not PV-specific — a residential wildland-urban-interface (WUI) home-survival forensic study; useful only as a defensible-space/structure-survival mechanism reference, not a solar loss benchmark. Flagged HAS_PATHWAY_ONLY pending primary-PDF re-fetch for exact table figures.

source_name: NIST Technical Note 2228 — fences and mulch WUI fire-spread experiments
solar_wildfire_use: DIRECT mechanism scope, not PV-specific: 187 experiments; fences spaced 91cm (3ft) apart still produced large flames, indicating close-spaced combustible fencing does not reliably prevent fire spread. Full quantitative tables not re-extracted from primary PDF in this pass.
has_open_numbers: 187 experiments; fences 91cm (3ft) apart still produced large flames.
does_not_have_open: Full primary-PDF quantitative tables; any PV-specific application.
notes: Not PV-specific — WUI fire-spread mechanism reference (fences/mulch as fire-spread pathways), useful for understanding defensible-space/setback effectiveness limits, not a solar loss benchmark.

source_name: NIST Technical Note 2205 — Hazard Mitigation Methodology (HMM) for WUI structures
solar_wildfire_use: DIRECT mechanism scope, not PV-specific: catalogs 40 vulnerabilities and 57 hardening options across 8 categories; Structure Separation Distance bands: Low Density >50ft, Moderate 25-50ft, High <25ft.
has_open_numbers: 40 vulnerabilities; 57 hardening options; 8 categories; Structure Separation Distance bands: Low Density >50ft, Moderate 25-50ft, High <25ft.
does_not_have_open: Any PV-specific hardening checklist; dollar figures for implementing hardening options.
notes: Not PV-specific — a general WUI structure-hardening methodology; the separation-distance bands are a useful mechanism/mitigation-state reference for a future solar-wildfire hardening checklist, not a loss benchmark.

source_name: Cohen 2000 — home ignitability and radiant heat flux modeling (SIAM)
solar_wildfire_use: DIRECT mechanism scope, not PV-specific: 20m x 50m flame-front model (SIAM); ignition not observed beyond 40m; at 40m, heat flux <20 kW/m^2 with minimum ignition time >10 minutes; minimum piloted ignition flux = 13 kW/m^2; flux-time ignition threshold = 11,500 (flux^2 * time, dimensionless SIAM correlation); structure survival rate 70% (nonflammable roof) vs 19% (flammable roof) per Foote 1994 as cited in Cohen 2000; 86% survival with >=10m clearance.
has_open_numbers: Heat flux <20 kW/m^2 at 40m; min ignition time >10min at that distance; min piloted ignition flux = 13 kW/m^2; flux-time threshold = 11,500; survival 70% (nonflammable roof) vs 19% (flammable roof); 86% survival with >=10m clearance.
does_not_have_open: Any PV-module-specific ignition threshold at distance; dollar figures.
notes: Not PV-specific — a wood-frame-home ignitability/distance-screening reference. Useful as a generic minimum-piloted-ignition-flux cross-check (13 kW/m^2) against the PV-material CHF figures (26 kW/m^2 module, 16.24 kW/m^2 cable) from Yang/Zhang, but structure type differs materially from solar hardware.

source_name: Syphard et al. 2014 — defensible-space effectiveness study, San Diego County
solar_wildfire_use: DIRECT mechanism scope, not PV-specific: effective defensible space found at 5-20m (never more than 30m of benefit observed); up to 40% woody-cover clearance effective; 60% cover retention still found effective; dataset of 687,869 homes, 4,315 destroyed 2001-2010 in San Diego County, matched sample of 1,000 destroyed + 1,000 surviving homes.
has_open_numbers: Effective defensible space 5-20m (max useful ~30m); up to 40% woody-cover clearance effective; 60% cover retention still effective; dataset: 687,869 homes, 4,315 destroyed (2001-2010, San Diego Co.), matched sample 1,000+1,000.
does_not_have_open: Any PV-specific defensible-space standard; dollar figures.
notes: Not PV-specific — a residential defensible-space effectiveness study; the 5-20m effective-distance finding is a useful mechanism/mitigation-state reference for evaluating vegetation-management standards at solar sites (directly relevant to the Australian inverter-fire/vegetation-management cluster in R3), not a loss benchmark.

source_name: DOE/FEMP — solar PV wildfire-hardening guidance
solar_wildfire_use: BOTH DIRECT and SMOKE-SOILING content (kept as separate rows/metric families). DIRECT: forest-fire temperatures ~1,470F (up to 2,000F in extreme conditions); material melting points (aluminum 1,220F, silicon 2,580F, silver 1,760F, glass 3,092F, steel 2,700F with 1,000F structural-failure onset); UL 1703 fire ratings Class A (10min @ ~1,400F, flame spread <=6ft) / Class B (10min, <=8ft) / Class C (4min, <=13ft); backsheet thickness 250-370 microns recommended >=300 microns; $16,000 anecdotal single-inverter replacement cost (200kW rooftop system, explicitly NOT a claims-based benchmark). SMOKE-SOILING (same source, separate rows): 9.4-37.8% production drop (PVROM, matches R3/OSTI figure); CA Sept 2020 10-30% statewide, up to 58% locally.
has_open_numbers: Forest-fire temps ~1,470F (up to 2,000F extreme); melt points: Al 1,220F, Si 2,580F, Ag 1,760F, glass 3,092F, steel 2,700F (1,000F structural failure onset); UL1703 Class A (10min@~1,400F,<=6ft)/B(10min,<=8ft)/C(4min,<=13ft); backsheet 250-370 microns, recommend >=300; $16,000 anecdotal inverter replacement (200kW rooftop); SMOKE-SOILING: 9.4-37.8% production drop (PVROM); CA Sept 2020: 10-30% statewide, up to 58% local.
does_not_have_open: A claims-based (vs. anecdotal) component-replacement cost; any AAL/PML solar-wildfire figure.
notes: The $16,000 anecdotal inverter-replacement figure is explicitly NOT a claims-based benchmark (single anecdotal data point, residential/small-commercial rooftop, not utility-scale) — must be flagged as such wherever cited. DIRECT (material/thermal/UL) and SMOKE-SOILING (9.4-37.8%/10-30%/58%) rows must be kept separate; the SMOKE-SOILING figures duplicate the same underlying PVROM dataset as OSTI_GILLETLY_WILDFIRE_PV_PRODUCTION_REGRESSION and ENERGY_GOV_FEMP_PVROM_WILDFIRE — not independent corroboration.

source_name: Gilletly/Jackson/Staid (OSTI/Sandia) — wildfire smoke PV production regression study
solar_wildfire_use: SMOKE-SOILING scope, primary benchmark candidate: PM2.5 concentrations of 50-200 ug/m^3 associated with 9.4%-37.8% reduction in normalized PV production; regression fit R^2=0.6732, RMSE=0.099; sample of 68 eligible sites (52 utility-scale + 16 distributed) across AZ/CA/ID/OR/UT; covers the 2018 Mendocino Complex (459,123 acres) and Camp Fire (153,336 acres) events.
has_open_numbers: 9.4%-37.8% reduction in normalized production at PM2.5 50-200 ug/m^3; R^2=0.6732, RMSE=0.099; 68 eligible sites (52 utility + 16 distributed), AZ/CA/ID/OR/UT; events: 2018 Mendocino Complex (459,123 acres), Camp Fire (153,336 acres).
does_not_have_open: Per-site (vs. pooled-regression) production-loss figures; $ revenue translation.
url: https://www.osti.gov/biblio/ (exact OSTI record ID pending verification)
notes: SMOKE-SOILING scope strictly; this is the PRIMARY quantified, peer-reviewed, utility-scale-inclusive open benchmark for smoke-driven PV generation shortfall (regression-fit, not anecdotal) — the same 9.4-37.8% figure is echoed (not independently re-derived) in ENERGY_GOV_FEMP_PVROM_WILDFIRE and DOE_FEMP_SOLAR_PV_WILDFIRE_HARDENING, all drawing on the same underlying PVROM dataset.

source_name: NREL — 'Impact of Wildfires on Solar Generation, Reserves, and Energy Prices' (snippet/webinar-derived extraction)
solar_wildfire_use: SMOKE-SOILING scope: 2020 wildfires caused up to 7.7% decrease in monthly PV generation (Sept most affected); +0.4%/+1.3% additional reductions in enhanced-wildfire/increased-PV-deployment scenarios; up to 5% (current conditions) / 6% (escalating-wildfire scenario) PV generation reduction alongside 53%/64% operating-reserve increases; webinar transcript cites daily PV generation reduction up to 19%. Direct PDF fetch failed (DNS error) in the research session; figures are snippet/webinar-transcript derived, flagged medium confidence pending re-fetch.

This is the SAME underlying NREL report as NREL_WILDFIRE_CAISO_TECH_REPORT_2023 (R3 entry) — cross-check rather than treat as independent corroboration; the 7.7% monthly figure matches across both entries.

source_name: Colorado State University study — solar resource availability under wildfire smoke, CONUS (Nature Communications, 2025)
solar_wildfire_use: SMOKE-SOILING scope, bounding/moderating counterpoint: confirms solar resource declines substantially under local optically-thick smoke (no specific % given for this regime in the extracted text), but transported/dilute smoke that extends across CONUS only minimally affects baseline monthly-mean GHI — decreases from transported smoke are only about 5%, and in some places less than 2%, versus the average in low-smoke years.
has_open_numbers: ~2-5% decrease in baseline monthly-mean GHI from transported/dilute smoke vs. low-smoke-year average (CONUS-wide); qualitative confirmation of 'substantial' declines under local optically-thick smoke (no specific % given).
does_not_have_open: A specific numeric bound for the 'local optically thick smoke' regime (described only qualitatively as 'large reductions'); PV-output-specific (vs. GHI-resource-specific) figure; $ translation.
notes: SMOKE-SOILING scope strictly. Critical scoping/bounding datum: shows the large (9-38%, up to 58% locally) SMOKE-SOILING figures from Gilletly/NREL/DOE-FEMP are event-proximate/local-smoke-specific, while the far more common regional-haze/transported-smoke exposure has a much smaller (~2-5%) baseline impact — the two regimes must not be conflated into a single point estimate.

source_name: NREL/Sandia/SunSpec — 'Best Practices in Photovoltaic System Operations and Maintenance', 2nd Edition
solar_wildfire_use: Zero wildfire, fire, or smoke content anywhere in this source (confirmed explicitly by extraction: 'No wildfire-related content is stated on the page'). Included only because the brief explicitly named it as a target for generic asset-decomposition/subsystem cost-share denominator context: O&M cost ~0.5%/year of system initial cost (large systems) / ~1%/year (small systems); ~25-year typical asset life; 100kW ground-mount fixed-tilt subsystem O&M cost breakdown (Inverter 31%, Cleaning/Vegetation 19%, PV Array 13%, Electrical 10%, Documents 8%, DC Wiring 5%, Mechanical 4%, PV Module 7%, AC Wiring 1%, Asset Management 2%, Monitoring/Meter 0%); comprehensive O&M raises performance 91%->95% average (83%->94% for underperforming lowest-quartile systems); 2020 residential O&M benchmark $28.94/kWdc/yr; broader guide cites $0-$40/kW/yr range, recommending standardization toward ~$10/kW/yr.
has_open_numbers: O&M cost ~0.5%/yr (large systems) / ~1%/yr (small systems) of initial cost; ~25-yr asset life; 100kW fixed-tilt O&M cost breakdown by subsystem (Inverter 31%, Cleaning/Vegetation 19%, PV Array 13%, Electrical 10%, Documents 8%, DC Wiring 5%, Mechanical 4%, PV Module 7%, AC Wiring 1%, Asset Mgmt 2%, Monitoring 0%); performance uplift 91%->95% (83%->94% underperforming); 2020 residential benchmark $28.94/kWdc/yr; broader range $0-$40/kW/yr, recommend ~$10/kW/yr.
does_not_have_open: Any wildfire, fire, or smoke content whatsoever — confirmed absent from this source.
notes: MUST NOT be cited as a wildfire mechanism source — contains zero fire/wildfire/smoke content. Included solely as the generic asset-decomposition/subsystem-cost-share denominator context the brief explicitly requested (e.g., inverter = 31% of O&M cost share), useful background for later damage-curve subsystem weighting, not wildfire evidence itself.

source_name: United States Large-Scale Solar Photovoltaic Database (USPVDB), version 4.0
solar_wildfire_use: Primary source population (facility polygons + AC/DC capacity, MW) to spatially intersect with wildfire-hazard-potential/burn-probability layers, producing the GW-in-high-wildfire-risk-zone denominator. 6,611 solar projects, 131,573 MW AC total rated capacity (v4.0, April 2026); prior versions: v3.0 (April 2025) 5,712 facilities; v1.0 (~2021) 3,699 facilities.
has_open_numbers: 6,611 solar projects, 131,573 MW AC total rated capacity, 49 states + DC (v4.0, April 2026); v3.0 (April 2025): 5,712 facilities; v1.0: 3,699 facilities (47 states + DC, through end of 2021).
does_not_have_open: No dollar value/TIV per facility (must be joined with the internal value-basis workbook, $1,120,000/MWdc installed TIV per the shared brief); no field verification; no wildfire-hazard attribute pre-joined (requires independent GIS overlay against USFS WHP/FSim or CAL FIRE FHSZ layers).
notes: The single most important exposure source for this pair — provides the facility-level MW denominator population, but carries zero wildfire-hazard or dollar-value attributes natively; a GIS join against source USFS_WHP_WRC_2024 / USFS_FSIM_RDS_2016_0034_3 / SOLAR_FHSZ_PROJECT_LEVEL_SCREENING would be required to produce a systematic GW-in-wildfire-risk-zone figure, which does not yet exist openly.

source_name: Form EIA-860 'Annual Electric Generator Report' (+ EIA-860M monthly update; EPM Table 6.1)
solar_wildfire_use: Secondary/cross-check denominator for state-level utility solar capacity (especially CA, TX, and other Western states with elevated wildfire hazard); underlying raw generator registry from which USPVDB is itself built. National total solar PV (utility+small-scale): 212,492.4 MW net summer capacity (Jan 2026), of which 152,542.3 MW utility-scale; California utility-scale solar ~13,000+ MW (June 2020); California small-scale solar 9,800 MW (mid-2020).
has_open_numbers: 212,492.4 MW total solar PV net summer capacity (Jan 2026: 152,542.3 MW utility + 59,950.1 MW small-scale); CA utility-scale ~13,000+ MW (June 2020); CA small-scale 9,800 MW (mid-2020); 2016 total 32,958 MW; 2017 total 41,356.8 MW.
does_not_have_open: No wildfire-hazard attribute; no risk-zone geocoding beyond plant lat/long (not pre-joined); no $ value data (capacity only).
notes: EIA-860 is the underlying raw generator registry from which USPVDB is built (not independent of source #1) — treat as a cross-check/more-frequently-updated companion, not a fully separate denominator source.

source_name: Wildfire Risk to Communities — national spatial datasets (Wildfire Hazard Potential, Burn Probability, Conditional Flame Length, Flame Length Exceedance Probability), 2024 version
solar_wildfire_use: National hazard-axis raster (30m resolution: annual burn probability 0-0.14, conditional flame length 0-861.7ft, flame-length-exceedance probability at 4ft/8ft thresholds) to spatially join against USPVDB polygons, producing per-facility or aggregate wildfire-hazard exposure scores/GW totals by hazard bin. Community/landscape-scale product, not locally calibrated; landscape conditions as of end of 2020.
has_open_numbers: Burn probability (BP) 0-0.14, 30m resolution; conditional flame length (CFL) 0-861.7 ft, 30m; flame-length exceedance probability at 4ft (FLEP4) and 8ft (FLEP8) thresholds; landscape conditions as of end of 2020.
does_not_have_open: No solar-facility attribute; no pre-built overlay product joining this hazard layer to USPVDB or EIA-860 solar sites was found in open literature (requires custom GIS join); no dollar-loss information whatsoever.
notes: 30m community-risk-oriented derivative of the coarser 270m national FSim burn-probability/FLP raster (USFS_FSIM_RDS_2016_0034_3) — same underlying FSim engine, different resolution/purpose. Explicitly 'not locally calibrated... best for considering risk as aggregated across a community rather than at the local, neighborhood, or individual home scale.'

source_name: Spatial datasets of probabilistic wildfire risk components for the United States (270m), 3rd Edition — cross-referenced in R5 exposure context
solar_wildfire_use: The exact dataset requested in the brief as the hazard-axis denominator: 6 discrete flame-length-probability bins (FLP1-FLP6), CONUS+AK+HI, 270m grid, landscape conditions as of end of 2020 (3rd edition, 2023 publication). Same underlying dataset as the R4 entry USFS_FSIM_RDS_2016_0034_3 (engineering/mechanism angle); this R5 entry documents its exposure/denominator role and version lineage (1st ed. 2016 CONUS-only -> 2nd ed. Short et al. 2020 adds AK/HI -> 3rd ed. 2023).
has_open_numbers: Burn probability (raster, per-270m-pixel annual probability) + 6 flame-length-probability bins: FLP1 <2ft, FLP2 2-4ft, FLP3 4-6ft, FLP4 6-8ft, FLP5 8-12ft, FLP6 12+ft; CONUS+AK+HI, landscape conditions end of 2020, 3rd edition (RDS-2016-0034-3).
does_not_have_open: No dollar-loss data of any kind; no solar-facility attribute; no pre-computed join against USPVDB was found publicly.
notes: DUPLICATE-BY-DESIGN cross-reference: this is the SAME underlying dataset as the R4 registry entry USFS_FSIM_RDS_2016_0034_3 — retained as a separate registry row (suffixed _R5) only to preserve the R5 file's distinct exposure/denominator framing and its version-lineage detail (1st/2nd/3rd edition history); do not double-count as independent evidence in the source_matrix or benchmark rows beyond noting the cross-reference.

source_name: CAL FIRE Fire Hazard Severity Zone (FHSZ) Viewer + individual utility-scale solar project environmental review documents
solar_wildfire_use: Illustrative, real-world confirmation that utility-scale solar projects are sited within CAL FIRE High/Very-High FHSZ zones, and a template for how a future systematic GW-in-FHSZ overlay could be constructed. Example: a 643-acre utility solar footprint (unnamed San Diego County project) explicitly geo-classified as majority High FHSZ, partially Moderate and Very High, per CAL FIRE 2007a data; Jacumba Solar cited as sited in High and Very High FHSZ.
has_open_numbers: 643 acres (single project footprint, majority High FHSZ, portion Moderate, small portion Very High, per CAL FIRE 2007a); Jacumba Solar project sited in High and Very High FHSZ zones (CAL FIRE FRAP 2014).
does_not_have_open: No statewide or national aggregation of 'GW of solar capacity in FHSZ Very-High/High zones' was located as a published, open, ready-made figure; no systematic USPVDB x FHSZ overlay study found in open literature.
notes: Explicitly downgraded to SCREENING_ONLY because it is anecdotal/project-level (acreage, not MW; not systematically compiled across all CA utility solar), not a systematic portfolio-wide GW total. A rigorous exposure figure would require an original GIS overlay of USPVDB against the CAL FIRE FHSZ layer — flagged as a data-request/DIY-GIS pathway, not fabricated.

source_name: National Interagency Fire Center — 'Wildfires and Acres' annual statistics
solar_wildfire_use: Pure context — establishes the scale of annual U.S. wildfire activity (frequency and acreage) against which any solar-specific claims frequency should be sanity-checked as an order-of-magnitude cross-check, never as a substitute benchmark. Zero linkage to solar assets, property value, or dollar loss.
does_not_have_open: No dollar-loss figures on this page; no solar/energy-infrastructure breakout; no breakdown by cause on this table.
notes: The single most commonly cited 'how big is the U.S. wildfire problem' statistic; explicitly downgraded R5 (GENERIC_ONLY) because it has zero linkage to solar assets, property value, or dollar loss.

source_name: National Interagency Fire Center — 'Suppression Costs' (federal firefighting costs, suppression only)
solar_wildfire_use: Background/context only — illustrates the annual scale of the federal wildfire-response budget; not usable for any solar loss normalization. A government cost outlay, not a property-loss or insured-loss figure.
does_not_have_open: Any property-loss or insured-loss figure; any state, facility, or asset-class breakout; any linkage to solar or any specific asset class.
notes: Suppression cost and property/structure loss are fundamentally different metric families (government operating expenditure vs. asset loss) — must never be summed or conflated with CAL FIRE/NOAA property-damage figures.

source_name: CAL FIRE Incident Archive (annual, by year) + 'Top 20 Most Destructive California Wildfires' table
solar_wildfire_use: Establishes California-specific context on fire frequency, acreage, and structure-destruction scale — a geography-matched (CA) generic denominator/context companion to national NIFC figures, since CA carries a large share of USPVDB utility solar capacity. Not usable as a solar-specific loss benchmark.
has_open_numbers: 2025 season: 16,627 structures destroyed statewide; Camp Fire (2018): 153,336 acres, 18,804 structures, 85 deaths; Tubbs Fire (2017): 36,807 acres, 5,636 structures, 22 deaths; Dixie Fire (2021): 963,309 acres, 1,311 structures (largest acreage in top-20); Palisades Fire (Jan 2025): 5,316 structures, 20,438 acres, 8 deaths; Eaton Fire (Jan 2025): 5,000+ structures, 13,690 acres, 16 deaths.
does_not_have_open: No dollar-value property-damage figures natively on CAL FIRE incident-archive pages (structures destroyed is a count, not a $ figure); no solar/energy-infrastructure line item in the structure-count definition.
notes: The brief's request for CAL FIRE '$ damage' could not be filled directly from CAL FIRE's own incident-archive pages — $ loss estimates for named fires (e.g., Palisades/Eaton) are sourced from insurer/reinsurer reports (see SWISSRE_SIGMA_NATCAT_2025, MUNICHRE_NATCAT_2025), not CAL FIRE directly. Gap noted rather than fabricated.

source_name: NOAA NCEI 'U.S. Billion-Dollar Weather and Climate Disasters' — wildfire event subset
solar_wildfire_use: National-level, multi-decade wildfire economic-loss context (all-property) — useful only as a macro sanity-check anchor (e.g., is a proposed solar-specific AAL/EAL figure a plausible small fraction of total national wildfire loss), never as a direct solar benchmark.
has_open_numbers: 17 US billion-dollar wildfire events 1980-2024, cumulative CPI-adjusted total $139.6bn; California Firestorm Summer-Fall 2018: $30.0bn CPI-adjusted (largest single event); CA/OR/WA Firestorms Fall 2020: $19.9bn; Western Wildfires 2021: $12.1bn; Hawaii Firestorm Aug 2023: $5.7bn.
does_not_have_open: No property-type breakout (residential vs. commercial vs. industrial/utility infrastructure) — cannot isolate any solar-specific share; no per-state/per-fire attribution within multi-state 'Western Wildfires' aggregate events; precise gross/net loss basis not laid out on the event table.
notes: Standard reference for aggregate U.S. wildfire economic loss, frequently cited in insurance/reinsurance commentary; explicitly excludes any solar/renewable-energy-specific breakdown and mixes many states/events within 'Western Wildfires' labels for a given year — downgraded to GENERIC_ONLY / R5.

source_name: Swiss Re Institute sigma — 'Natural catastrophes in 2025: the persistent rise of wildfire and storm losses'
solar_wildfire_use: Provides the current 'state of the art' reinsurance-industry benchmark for how large a single wildfire insured-loss event can be (record $40bn) — useful only as macro context/ceiling reference, not as a solar-specific number; explicitly residential-dominated per Swiss Re's own framing.
has_open_numbers: California wildfires (Palisades + Eaton, Jan 2025): USD 40 billion insured losses, the largest-ever insured wildfire loss event, destroying more than 16,000 structures; global insured NatCat total 2025: USD 107 billion; secondary perils (wildfire/storm/flood) = record 92% of global insured NatCat losses in 2025.
does_not_have_open: Any asset-class breakout (residential vs. commercial vs. utility/energy infrastructure) within the $40bn LA wildfire figure — text explicitly frames the loss as concentrated in 'high-value single-family residential property'; no gross-vs-net distinction stated.
notes: Reinsurance-industry record-setting figure for wildfire, corroborated by MUNICHRE_NATCAT_2025 for the same January 2025 LA wildfire event — entirely a residential/general-property loss figure with zero solar attribution.

source_name: Munich Re — 'Climate change presses on: Devastating wildfires and intense thunderstorms exacerbate losses for insurers' (2025 annual natural-disaster figures)
solar_wildfire_use: Cross-checks and corroborates the Swiss Re figure for the same January 2025 LA wildfire event; together the two reinsurer reports establish the current global ceiling/record for a single wildfire insured-loss event, for macro context only.
has_open_numbers: Palisades + Eaton fires (LA, Jan 2025): overall loss ~US$53 billion, of which ~US$40 billion insured — the most expensive wildfire disaster to date, 30 lives lost; global 2025 insured NatCat losses (all perils): US$108 billion, with the LA wildfires the single costliest event.
does_not_have_open: Any asset-class or peril-mechanism breakout (direct flame damage vs. smoke vs. business interruption) within the $53bn/$40bn figures; any solar or energy-infrastructure-specific line item.
notes: Munich Re's $53bn total-loss/$40bn insured-loss split for the same event as Swiss Re's $40bn insured-only figure shows close cross-reinsurer agreement, increasing confidence in that headline number as a market-consensus figure — but it remains entirely a residential/general-property loss figure with zero solar attribution.
